column2: — Oh no.
Well, you determine who gets what.
It's like a coin toss, when there are more participants, — Lilichka sincerely explained the rules to Greg.
— You're all insane over there!
— Greg shouted.
— Hear that?
— Lilichka whispered to Miyechka, pressing the phone to her chest.
— Hear it?
That's Nati speaking in him right now.
— My mother and I, — Greg shouted, — decided long ago that we could take in another girl.
— Greg, what does your mother have to do with this?
It's not her child.
— Tell him that maybe Ivan will change his mind, — Miyechka whispered.
— And anyway, on paper she's Ivan's daughter, — said Lilichka.
That calmed Greg down a little.
Lilichka hung up and sat down.
The day was lazy.
Marta was mixing lemonade.
So sour that no one but her could drink it.
Lusya was driving little cars around in the sand.
Maria was drinking the wine she'd brought from Greece, light as April air.
Something kept buzzing and flying about, clouds hung overhead.
Real summer had arrived.
Marta took a glass of lemonade and came over to let Lusya try it.
Lusya wanted to take the glass with her sandy hands, but Marta wouldn't let her.
Instead the little girl stretched out her lips, trying to get herself some lemonade without touching the glass with her hands.
Lusya didn't make a face and asked for more, reaching for the glass, and Marta glanced triumphantly at her sisters.
— She's her little one, — said the grandmother.
The Orchard
Miyechka got up early, when the sun was only just beginning to rise, and quietly stole through the room where Maria slept.
The sun was not yet shining through the windows.
In the garden it was still gray and dim.
Wet branches tapped against the windows.
The grandmother always said that this garden would one day eat them all up itself.
It really did become more independent every day and served its owners less and less.
The plums had long been out of reach.
They ripened somewhere up there, and then fell on people's heads, already half-rotten, and were meekly raked up with shovels.

Once the grandfather had made plum brandy from them; now they just hung there menacingly, so that later that far corner of the yard, where the life of the windfall fruit ended, would smell of plum brandy.
The pears too hung high and menacingly.
With the pear tree you had to play a game: it would throw down ripe fruit, and you had to manage to eat them before they rotted.
The pear tree most often won.
The raspberry had long been a separate kingdom — into the very middle of the thickets, where those undegenerated Tashkent berries the size of a finger still hung, no one could enter anymore.
People meekly ate it away from various sides, while there, in the depths, 'wild boars have probably bred piglets by now,' the grandmother would say.
The house held its defenses as best it could.
The wet tree branches tapped against the windows.
It was morning.
In the dark kitchen, on the windowsill, Miyechka found a pen — it was the only proof that a telephone had once stood on that windowsill.
The telephone had long been gone, but the pen had stayed put.
Miyechka wrote on a napkin that she'd gone for a walk, left it on the table, and went outside.
Miyechka hadn't even reached the gate before her feet got wet in her sneakers.
She unlaced her shoes, took them in her hands and walked on barefoot.
The grass was cold.
Then she stopped, took out her phone and photographed her bare feet on the grass.
— Why did you get up so early?
— Ustym wrote.
— I'm going to look at the land, — Miyechka texted back.
It was about twenty minutes' walk to the land, and Miyechka walked at a brisk pace along the paths, edged with soft knotgrass.
When you walk barefoot, for some reason every step echoes in your head, so she walked a little on tiptoe, then ran, then walked at a brisk pace again.
In the village you wouldn't meet anyone anyway, but just in case she went down to the path that ran below the vegetable gardens.

Enormous alders and ash trees grew over the path, and she ran with such pleasure as she had never felt running, neither in Hyde Park in her new pink Nike sneakers, nor along the Venetian canals, nor by the sea, when the sun warmed her shoulders pleasantly.
She felt hungry, plucked a wild-grown apple from beside the path and ate it as she walked.
A path led to the land across a small strip of field.
At last the apple trees came into view.
Miyechka came up to them.
Some looked still young, others bent toward the ground, and their branches were gray with rough bark grown over them.
She squeezed between the apple trees and saw what even rows they were planted in.
From the road you could see only a chaotic, overgrown forest.
Miyechka remembered how she and Danylo had gone to Scotland, where the hills were overgrown with soft grass, where it was wet, windy and spacious.
He stood then buttoned up in his jacket to the very tip of his nose, fine drops settled from the mist lay on his hair.
He walked cautiously, checking every step.
And she, for some reason, took offense, and now could no longer remember why.
Probably she just wanted to throw him off balance, so that those soft hills would tremble with fury.
Instead the fury grew only in her, while he was, as always, calm and simply asked: 'Tired, little bird?'
In the orchard apples had set thickly on the trees.
Miyechka plucked a small one with a red side.
Sour, like Marta's lemonade.
She took her phone, photographed the apple trees.
Some rows were completely clear, others were so overgrown with self-seeded saplings and weeds that only the tops of the apple trees could be seen.
'How to clear an apple orchard,' Miyechka typed into Google.
There was an ad for a sanatorium, videos on how to graft apple trees, how to prune branches, but about what equipment you could use to clear such an orchard there was nothing.
She googled in French — there were pictures of young apple trees and village markets, where old men with bird-like faces sold apples from woven baskets.
Miyechka went up to one of the apple trees and pulled toward herself a young sapling growing by the trunk.
Despite the ground being wet from the rains that hadn't stopped all weekend, the sapling did not give way.
'What is to be done with all this?'
— thought Miyechka.
She pictured a clean orchard with old apple trees, like the ones she'd once seen on the northern French mountain slopes.
She sent Ustym the photo of the orchard, raising her hand so the photo would send faster.
It was a habit from childhood, when there was still no cell tower, so that to talk to family or loved ones everyone would climb up trees, cellars and windowsills.
Mia looked over the photo she'd sent.
The apple trees stood in tall green grass, the morning mist still spread between them, clinging to the bushes that had grown up around the trunks.
'Green with gray,' Miyechka thought, looking at the photo, 'what a beautiful combination, I should buy myself something like that one day.'
The first slanting rays of sun scattered in the rows between the trees.
The little spikelets on the grass that no one had mowed for decades glowed in the dull, wet greenery.
— You have to uproot it, — lit up in a message.
Miyechka frowned and turned off the phone.
'If you love trees, you have to cut down the old ones and plant new ones,' the grandfather had said when she and Lilichka were sobbing over the old poplar, watching it being sawn down.
It was autumn, the poplar was green-yellow, and, as it was sawn, leaves flew off it.

And then it crashed down helplessly onto the grass-overgrown road to their yard and lay there like an enormous whale that had beached itself, and it's clear to everyone that it will never return to the sea.
She had once seen a whale, too.

Then she immediately remembered the poplar, and understood the futility of the efforts of those people who tried to splash water on the whale, while it lay, a black carcass, on the yellow autumn sand.
Miyechka understood that it was the same as if she and Lilichka, back when they were seven and eight, had tried to lift that poplar lying across their road and set it back onto its stump.
Later they did the same with the whale as with the poplar — sawed it into pieces and took it away, so it wouldn't block anyone's road.
Later, Miyechka read in the local paper, its skeleton was taken to Madrid, where it may lie even now.

And the poplar was sawn into boards, and now Lusya walks on them with her bare little feet — they were used to make the floor in the farthest room, adjoining Marta's, where Lusya slept, her little one, who had come to her after the game with the matches.
'It has to be uprooted,' Miyechka said to herself as she walked out of the orchard.
The sun was higher now and shone on her back.
'Everyone immediately wants to uproot.'
It was getting hot, the morning freshness was wearing off, the mist had dispersed, and with every minute the day became more ordinary, simpler, but for that reason cozier too.
Miyechka walked and greeted everyone along the way, no longer hiding on the path above the river, no one could ruin the magic of the morning anymore, because its spell had already dispersed.
Approaching the house, she remembered that long ago it had been visible from the road.
You could see the steps, and on the steps — the chair where the grandmother often sat.
Now only the roof was visible.
And one window.
Behind that window Marta was supposed to be sleeping.
By the water Miyechka saw a boy playing, with curly hair, exactly like that of the imaginary girl she used to lead by the hand around the city.
She entered the house through the other entrance, through the little room where Lusya slept.
The door was ajar, so the cool morning air would come into the house.
Miyechka looked at the sleeping little child, at the little chest rising with her breathing, at the sunken little belly, at the bony little knees.
She lay down very quietly beside Lusya, touched her little finger with her own.
The sun was not yet shining through the window, but had already entered the garden.
Miyechka thought how good it must be when you're pregnant and then nothing worries you, because you're carrying out one very important mission.
No need to write, no need to read, no need for anything, there's just one supremely important task.
She must tell Marta about this.
Then Ustym appeared before her eyes, and his smile, and the way he'd told Mrs. Darochka about the blessing, and then how she'd seen him off in the rain and held her hand on his ribs.
Miyechka took Lusya by the hand and didn't notice herself falling asleep.
She woke up already without the child at her side.

Lusya had opened her eyes earlier, sat up on the bed, carefully slid off the sofa, avoiding Miyechka's legs, which she'd tucked under herself, curled up in a ball, and went to the kitchen, where the grandmother sat peeling potatoes for breakfast.
At first they waited for Miyechka to come back from the land, and only around ten did the grandmother come in to her, to close the door so flies wouldn't fly into the sunny, warm little room.
— And who's this here?
— she asked.
— Granny...
— Miyechka drawled, shielding herself from the sun with her hand.
Miyechka had grown sweaty under the sun shining through the window.
Her neck was damp, little drops had appeared on her forehead.
— We were already thinking of sending out an expedition after you, — said the grandmother.
— Granny, we have to tell Marta to focus only on her pregnancy, — retorted Miyechka, stretching.
— She's very focused as it is, — replied the grandmother.
— Just try not to focus when you're throwing up all day long…
— It's already the seventh month, why is she still nauseous?
— I don't know, sweetheart, she's got two in there.
— They say that orchard needs to be uprooted, — said Miyechka and sat up on the bed.
— There you have it, — replied the grandmother.
— First plant, then uproot.
I always said that farming is the most thankless business.

Grow, sell for a pittance, grow again… It rots, gets overgrown, insects and caterpillars devour it, cattle trample it, it dries up, and if once in a while something nice grows, then — sell it for a pittance anyway, because everyone got a good crop…
— So you think — uproot it?
— Miyechka got to her feet and stretched.
She was wearing a tank top with Minnie Mouse — the Disney mouse with the bow.
— What do you need it for, Solomiya?
— Oh, just look at this, — Miyechka showed the grandmother the photo on the phone.
— I won't see anything there, — said the grandmother, squinting in the sun.
— I'll print it out for you.
Miyechka looked again at the photo of the orchard — at that morning mist and the golden spikelets in the rows.
— Mia, what do you need those apple trees for?
— asked Lilichka, who had come up to the room by the steps from the garden.
Lilichka looked tired.
Her eyes were small, as if she hadn't slept all night.
— What's wrong with you?
— asked Solomiya.
— Roberto and Marko got into a fight.
— Who beat whom?
— I don't know, Greg is very upset.
— And Nati?
— Nati's not upset.
— And why is Greg?
— He doesn't like it when they fight.
— And why are you?
— Well, you understand that Marko isn't anyone's own there.
Only mine, and I'm not there.
— Oh, Nati loves Marko more than Roberto.
— As long as everything's peaceful — maybe.
Listen, leave those apple trees alone.
Let them grow there by themselves…
— And what am I to do?
— What are you best at?
— Nothing.
That evening the three of them, leaving Lusya at home, went to the doctor.
Marta had been gloomy since morning, someone kept calling her, she'd reject the calls, and then she threw the phone under the clothes in the wardrobe and ordered no one to touch it.
Everyone at home thought it was the father of her children calling.
Marta was furious that everyone had guessed.
But no one said a word, so there were no formal grounds to shout at anyone.
Marta felt sick again after breakfast, and in the afternoon she, along with Miyechka and Lilichka, the three of them, went to the doctor.

The doctor, seeing Marta's expression, asked no unnecessary questions, but silently moved the sensor over her belly and said that the one on the right was a bit bigger, and the left one seemed somewhat smaller.
After the clinic the three of them went into a café.
Marta was stirring green tea, Miyechka and Lilichka — coffee with cream.
Suddenly a girl got up from a table at the other end of the room and came over to them.
Her hands were painted with henna, her sun-bleached hair gathered in a bun on the very top of her head.
A yellow tank top and wide trousers dangled on her.
— Solomiya!
— she exclaimed.
